name: azure-identity-ts
description: Authenticate to Azure services using Azure Identity SDK for JavaScript (@azure/identity). Use when configuring authentication with DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, or interactive browser login.
capabilities: Configure DefaultAzureCredential for automatic auth method selection
Set up managed identity for Azure-hosted applications
Implement service principal authentication with client secrets or certificates
Use developer credentials (Azure CLI, Azure PowerShell, Azure Developer CLI)
Configure custom credential chains with fallback options
Handle sovereign cloud authentication (Azure Government, Azure China)
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a documentation skill for Azure Identity SDK. Static findings are false positives - the skill documents authentication methods (environment variables, CLI commands, certificate paths) which are standard for authentication library documentation. No executable code, no credential handling, only documentation with placeholder values.
valueStatement: This skill provides documentation and examples for authenticating to Azure services using the Azure Identity SDK for JavaScript and TypeScript. It supports multiple authentication methods including managed identities, service principals, and developer credentials.
limitations: This is a documentation skill only - provides reference examples, not runtime functionality
Does not handle credential lifecycle management or token caching
Does not provide MFA or conditional access configuration
Requires Azure subscription and proper IAM roles for use

useCases.targetUser: Azure developers deploying apps to Azure
useCases.title: Azure App Service Authentication
useCases.description: Configure managed identity authentication for a Node.js app running on Azure App Service to access Azure Storage

useCases.title: CI/CD Pipeline Authentication
useCases.description: Set up service principal authentication for automated deployments without interactive login

useCases.targetUser: Software developers working with Azure SDKs locally
useCases.title: Local Development Setup
useCases.description: Use Azure CLI credential for local development with same authentication flow as production

promptTemplates.title: Basic Azure Authentication
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I authenticate to Azure using DefaultAzureCredential in my TypeScript project?
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting started with Azure SDK authentication

promptTemplates.title: Managed Identity Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to use managed identity to authenticate from an Azure VM
promptTemplates.scenario: Configuring system-assigned or user-assigned managed identity

promptTemplates.title: Service Principal Auth
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I authenticate using a service principal with client secret?
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up non-interactive authentication for applications

promptTemplates.title: Custom Credential Chain
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a custom credential chain that tries managed identity first, then falls back to Azure CLI
promptTemplates.scenario: Building resilient authentication with multiple fallback options

bestPractices: Use DefaultAzureCredential in most cases - it automatically selects the best auth method
Prefer managed identity in production to avoid storing secrets
Use ChainedTokenCredential to create custom fallback chains for reliability
antiPatterns: Hardcoding client secrets in source code - use environment variables instead
Using interactive credentials in production - use managed identity or service principals
Ignoring credential chain failures - always configure proper fallback options

faq.question: What is DefaultAzureCredential?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential is a chain that tries multiple authentication methods in order: EnvironmentCredential, WorkloadIdentityCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, VisualStudioCodeCredential, AzureCliCredential, AzurePowerShellCredential, and AzureDeveloperCliCredential. It works automatically in both development and production.

faq.question: When should I use managed identity?
faq.answer: Use managed identity when your application runs on Azure services that support it (App Service, VMs, AKS, Functions). It eliminates the need to manage secrets and is the recommended authentication method for production Azure workloads.

faq.question: How do I authenticate locally during development?
faq.answer: Use AzureCliCredential, AzurePowerShellCredential, or AzureDeveloperCliCredential for local development. Run 'az login', 'Connect-AzAccount', or 'azd auth login' once to authenticate, and the SDK will use those credentials.

faq.question: What is the difference between service principal and managed identity?
faq.answer: A service principal is an application registration in Azure AD that you create and manage manually. Managed identity is an identity automatically created by Azure for resources like VMs or App Services. Managed identity requires no secret management.

faq.question: Can I use certificates for authentication?
faq.answer: Yes, use ClientCertificateCredential with a certificate path and optional password, or set AZURE_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_PATH environment variable. This is more secure than client secrets for service principal authentication.

faq.question: How do I handle sovereign clouds?
faq.answer: Use the authorityHost option with AzureAuthorityHosts (AzureGovernment, AzureChina) when creating credentials. Pass it in the credential constructor options.
